glossary.def: Time-respecting cross-validation for forecasting: fit on data up to an origin t, forecast the next h steps, score them, then slide the origin forward and repeat. An expanding window keeps all history (best when stationary); a sliding window of fixed length forgets old data (best when the process drifts). The model at origin t may use nothing dated after t.

The benchmark that copies the last observed value forward (the seasonal variant copies one season back). It is the yardstick every model must beat, and the denominator of MASE; a model that cannot beat it is not worth deploying.

Mean Absolute Percentage Error: the average of |actual − forecast| / |actual|, expressed as a percent. Scale-free and intuitive, but it explodes near zero actuals, is undefined at zero, and penalizes over-forecasts more than under-forecasts (asymmetric).

Mean Absolute Scaled Error (Hyndman & Koehler, 2006): the model's mean absolute error divided by the in-sample mean absolute error of the one-step naive forecast. It is unitless, defined even at zero, and symmetric; MASE < 1 means the model beats the naive forecast, MASE > 1 means it loses to copy-the-last-value.

Symmetric Mean Absolute Percentage Error: |actual − forecast| divided by the average of |actual| and |forecast|, averaged and scaled to percent. Bounded and less one-sided than MAPE, but only approximately symmetric, undefined when both values are zero, and convention-dependent (some forms cap at 100%, others at 200%).

The fraction of realized outcomes that fall inside a model's prediction intervals. A calibrated 90% interval covers the truth about 90% of the time; coverage well below nominal means over-confident (too-tight) intervals — the common failure — while coverage above nominal means needlessly wide bands.

A distribution-free wrapper that turns any point forecaster into one with finite-sample coverage guarantees, by calibrating interval widths on held-out residuals under an exchangeability assumption. A practical alternative to trusting a Gaussian error model for prediction intervals.

Letting any post-origin information into a pre-origin decision — scaling or imputing across the train/test seam, using revised figures known only after the target, or tuning on the test set. It inflates offline scores into fiction; the fix is to fit every transform inside each fold on past data alone and simulate production exactly.
